broken_query: SELECT id name, email, created_at FROM users;
correct_query: SELECT id, name, email, created_at FROM users;
description: Missing comma between 'id' and 'name' in the SELECT clause causes a syntax error.
expected_improvement: Query parses and executes successfully, returning all four columns from the users table.

broken_query: SELECT e.name, e.salary, d.name AS department FROM employees e, departments d;
correct_query: SELECT e.name, e.salary, d.name AS department FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id;
description: No JOIN condition between employees and departments produces a cartesian product, multiplying every employee row by every department row.
expected_improvement: Adding the ON clause restricts results to correct employee–department pairs, eliminating the cartesian product and returning accurate data.

broken_query: SELECT a.id, a.name, (SELECT SUM(p.view_count) FROM posts p WHERE p.author_id = a.id) AS total_views FROM authors a ORDER BY total_views DESC LIMIT 10;
correct_query: SELECT a.id, a.name, SUM(p.view_count) AS total_views FROM authors a JOIN posts p ON a.id = p.author_id GROUP BY a.id, a.name ORDER BY total_views DESC LIMIT 10;
description: A correlated subquery in the SELECT clause re-executes a full scan of the posts table once per author row, making the query O(n²) and extremely slow on large datasets.
expected_improvement: Rewriting with a JOIN and GROUP BY computes the aggregate in a single pass over the posts table, dramatically reducing execution time and resource usage.
